The paper systematically labels token-level exact-match scores as 'execution accuracy' throughout — including in the abstract claim, per-benchmark results, and the concluding sentence. Exact-match and execution accuracy are distinct metrics; execution accuracy requires running the SQL against a live database and comparing result sets, not string-matching canonicalized query text. This conflation propagates through every quantitative claim and invalidates the stated conclusion that decomposition produces queries whose 'execution behavior more closely matches user intent.'","R1.3":"Centrally expected. The paper presents concrete benchmark numbers (78.4% Spider, 86.1% WikiSQL, 54.0% BIRD) against named baselines (T5-3B, 'prior reported best'). Verifying that these figures are internally consistent with the described protocol, that baseline comparisons are apples-to-apples (same split, same metric), and that the Section 6 stratification claim is supported by presented evidence is a natural obligation under 'check this.'"},"clarification_needed":true,"clarification_question":"Should I check for technical and methodological soundness (metric labeling, claim-evidence alignment, protocol validity, baseline comparability) or for writing and presentation quality?"}

rationale.R1.1: The artifact advances a specific contribution claim (schema-grounded decomposition improves end-to-end accuracy); checking whether that thesis is coherently and accurately framed is within plausible scope of 'check this' on a technical evaluation passage.
rationale.R1.2: Load-bearing. The paper systematically labels token-level exact-match scores as 'execution accuracy' throughout — including in the abstract claim, per-benchmark results, and the concluding sentence. Exact-match and execution accuracy are distinct metrics; execution accuracy requires running the SQL against a live database and comparing result sets, not string-matching canonicalized query text. This conflation propagates through every quantitative claim and invalidates the stated conclusion that decomposition produces queries whose 'execution behavior more closely matches user intent.'
rationale.R1.3: Centrally expected. The paper presents concrete benchmark numbers (78.4% Spider, 86.1% WikiSQL, 54.0% BIRD) against named baselines (T5-3B, 'prior reported best'). Verifying that these figures are internally consistent with the described protocol, that baseline comparisons are apples-to-apples (same split, same metric), and that the Section 6 stratification claim is supported by presented evidence is a natural obligation under 'check this.'

parsed.clarification_question: Should I check for technical and methodological soundness (metric labeling, claim-evidence alignment, protocol validity, baseline comparability) or for writing and presentation quality?
